title: Switch an LED from your phone
learnerProfile: adult beginner; curious and capable; no electronics assumed
mission: Wire one LED to GPIO 2, upload the Bluetooth sketch, and switch the light from your phone — write led_on and led_off in LightBlue and watch a value sent over the air trip a callback on the board and drive the pin. Today your words close a control loop with no wire between you and the board.

sourceMetadata.imageAlt: Official Freenove circuit for Bluetooth Control LED: a single LED on GPIO 2 through a 220 ohm resistor to ground, shown as both a schematic and a breadboard photo.
sourceMetadata.licenseNote: Based on Freenove official material released under CC BY-NC-SA 3.0; preserve attribution and non-affiliation language.

parts.name: ESP32-S3 board
parts.explanation: A development board is a friendly package around a tiny computer chip. The ESP32-S3 runs your uploaded sketch, controls pins, reads sensors, and can also use USB, Wi-Fi, and Bluetooth. In this lesson, it sends the ping signal and measures the echo time.

parts.name: GPIO extension board
parts.explanation: GPIO means general-purpose input/output. These pins are connection points your code can use. The extension board makes them easier to see, reach, and wire without cramming everything onto the ESP32-S3 itself.

parts.name: LED
parts.explanation: A LED is a one-way light. It only lights when its longer leg (the +) faces the pin and its shorter leg (the −) faces ground. Too much current burns it out, so a LED always sits behind a resistor.

parts.name: 220 Ω resistor
parts.explanation: A resistor is a small component whose whole job is to limit how much current flows through a wire. Current is the flow of electricity through the circuit; a resistor throttles that flow down to a safe amount. Its strength is measured in ohms (Ω) — a larger number resists harder and lets less current through. A part like a LED wired straight to power pulls far more current than it can survive and burns out at once, so it sits in series with a resistor whose value is chosen to hold the current gentle enough to keep it alive. The coloured bands print the resistor's value, and it works the same either way round — a resistor has no positive or negative end.

parts.name: 2 jumper wires (M/M)
parts.explanation: Jumper wires make temporary connections without soldering. Female-to-male wires have a socket on one end and a pin on the other, useful when a sensor module and board use different connector styles.

parts.name: Arduino IDE
parts.explanation: Arduino IDE is the desktop app that opens the sketch, compiles it, uploads it to the ESP32-S3, and shows messages from the board in Serial Monitor.

wiring.reason: The pin a matched command drives HIGH or LOW.
Completes the LED's path back to zero volts.

coachInstructions: Guide one physical step at a time and wait for learner confirmation before proceeding.

Teach the core loop — the phone writes a value into the write characteristic, a callback the sketch registered fires on arrival and copies it into rxload, then the loop matches it and calls digitalWrite; the arrival wakes the board, which is what makes it event-driven control.
The commands are led_on and led_off with underscores, written as utf-string; the manual's captions show hyphens and are wrong — always follow the code, never print the hyphen form.
If the LED never reacts, check Serial Monitor at 115200 first — an echoed command with a dark LED points at wiring on GPIO 2; no echo points at the phone side or the spelling.
If the LED is wired but dead, confirm long leg to GPIO 2 through the 220 Ω resistor and short leg to GND before changing code.
If the phone can't find the board, repeat Day 27's LightBlue connection steps and have the learner press reset before reconnecting.
Keep the Arduino path primary; the MicroPython version's on_rx is the same callback, it broadcasts as ESP32S3, and it matches each command byte-for-byte, so expect those differences.
steps: Seat the ESP32-S3 on the GPIO extension board and keep USB unplugged while you wire.
Place the LED so its long leg (+) is on the GPIO 2 side and its short leg (−) heads toward ground.
Put the 220 Ω resistor in series between GPIO 2 and the LED's long leg.
Compare every wire to the chart, then plug in USB.
Open Sketch_20.2_BluetoothToLed.ino in Arduino IDE and upload it.
Open Serial Monitor at 115200 and look for the "device started" line.

On your phone, open LightBlue (https://apps.apple.com/us/app/lightblue/id557428110) (Android (https://play.google.com/store/apps/details?id=com.punchthrough.lightblueexplorer)) and connect to ESP32S3_Bluetooth, exactly as you did on Day 27.
Find the write characteristic, set the format to utf-string, and write led_on — underscore, exact spelling.
Watch the LED light, then write led_off to put it out.

arduino.explanation: Starts the same radio as Day 27 and registers the callback that fires on every write, so an arriving command lands in rxload without the loop asking for it.
Reads the mailbox and tests whether the first six characters spell led_on, underscore included — the match is what turns a received value into a command.
The matched command drives GPIO 2 HIGH and the LED lights; led_off drives it LOW — the moment the phone's word becomes a voltage.
Echoes each received command to Serial Monitor, so you can watch the word arrive at the same instant the LED obeys.

micropython.explanation: The on_rx function IS the callback here — it compares the whole received bytes to the exact command, where the Arduino sketch checks only the front of the buffer.
Drives GPIO 2 HIGH and the LED lights.

theoryModel.plainLanguage: A value the phone writes into the board's mailbox trips a callback, and the arriving bytes drive the pin — a control loop closed over the air.
theoryModel.formula: phone writes the characteristic → callback fires → match a known word → digitalWrite

notes.title: Woken by the arrival
notes.body: The radio holds your callback and fires it the instant a write lands, so the board spends its time ready and acts only when a message comes. That is event-driven control, and it scales — one board can hold many callbacks, each waiting for its own message.

notes.title: Why spelling is the whole protocol
notes.body: The board compares raw characters. led_on with an underscore is a command; any other spelling is text that arrives, gets echoed, and leaves the LED where it was. The meaning lives in the match, so both ends must agree on the words.

notes.title: The radio only carries bytes
notes.body: BLE moves the characters and nothing more. Which words count as commands, and what each one does, lives entirely in the sketch — the same bytes could dim a light, log a reading, or steer a motor.

expectedOutputExample: Write led_on in LightBlue (utf-string format) — the LED lights.
Write led_off — the LED goes dark.
Any other text leaves the LED exactly as it was — the sketch only acts on its two known commands.
test.successCriteria: Serial Monitor at 115200 shows "The device started, now you can pair it with Bluetooth!" on boot, then echoes every command the board receives.

firstChecks: Send the underscore spelling led_on exactly, in utf-string format.
Long leg to GPIO 2 through the 220 Ω resistor, short leg to GND.
Repeat Day 27's LightBlue steps, and press reset before reconnecting.

challenge: Send several values from LightBlue and watch which ones become actions, then add a PWM branch that reads the received value as a brightness so the phone dials the light.
